ccel.title: NPNF-213. Gregory the Great (II), Ephraim Syrus, Aphrahat

source.title: NPNF-213. Gregory the Great (II), Ephraim Syrus, Aphrahat

title: To Januarius, Bishop of Caralis (Cagliari).

source.title: NPNF-213. Gregory the Great (II), Ephraim Syrus, Aphrahat

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Gregory to Januarius, a Bishop of Sardinia.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: We knew before the letter of your Fraternity reached us what our enemies had effected in Sardinia.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And, having for some time feared that this would be so, we now groan with you on what we foresaw having come to pass.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But, if attention had been paid to what we wrote to our most excellent son Gennadius, as well as to yourself, telling you that this would be so, the enemy would either not have come into your regions, or, when they came, they would have incurred the danger which they have caused.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Even now, then, let what has happened sharpen your vigilance for the future.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For we, too, by no means omit whatever we are able to do for good, the Lord helping us.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Know, moreover, that the abbot whom, now a considerable time ago, we sent to Agilulph, has by the mercy of God arranged a peace with him, so far as was directed in writing by the most excellent Exarch.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And so, till such time as the agreements for the confirmation of this peace shall be drawn up, lest perchance our enemies during the present delay should be inclined to come again into those parts, do you cause watches of the walls to be kept up, and careful attention given in all places.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And we trust in the power of our Redeemer that the incursions or plots of our adversaries will not injure you anew.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: As to your saying in your letter that many persons lay complaints against you before us, this is true; but among various things nothing has distressed us so much as what our most beloved son, the abbot Cyriacus, has reported to us; namely, that on the Lord’s day before mass you caused a crop of corn to be ploughed up in the field which is in the possession of Donatus, and, as if that were not enough, went, after the sacrifice was finished, in person to the place, and dug up the boundaries.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For this reason I exhort thee to consider with anxious attention the office which thou bearest, and to avoid entirely whatever may injure thy reputation or thy soul, and let no one persuade thee to do the like again.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For know that thou hast not undertaken the care of earthly things, but the leadership of souls.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: On this, therefore, thou oughtest to fix thy heart, thy anxiety, thy entire devotion., and to give thy diligent thought to the winning of souls, that when thou shalt render to the Lord at His coming the talents that He has delivered to thee multiplied, thou mayest be counted worthy to receive from Him the fruit of retribution, and to be exalted among His faithful servants in eternal glory.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Know, however, that what I now say in the way of reproach or blame comes not from asperity, but from brotherly love, since I desire thee to be found a priest before Almighty God, not in name only, which tends only to punishment, but also in desert, which looks to recompense.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For, we being one member in the body of our Redeemer, as I am rent asunder in thy fault, so also am I rejoiced in thy good conduct.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Furthermore, with regard to your desire that we should depute a person from our side (a nostro latere), to whom you may communicate in detail the cases that are to be referred to us, write whatever you will to our most beloved son Peter and to Theodore the counsellor (consiliario), that, when it has been communicated to us through them, whatever reason may commend may be settled, the Lord revealing the way.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Moreover, concerning our brother and fellow-bishop Marinianus, cognizance will be taken, when peace with the aforesaid Agilulph shall have been fully confirmed, and whatever the order of reason may dictate will be done.
